label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking when an ordinary tropical storm is called a hurricane. The options are A, B, C, D. I need to find the correct answer based on the passage provided. First, I'll read the passage again to find the relevant part. The first paragraph says: "many powerful storms are born in the tropical Atlantic and Caribbean seas. Of these, only about half a dozen becomes the strong, circling winds of 75 miles per hour or more that are called hurricanes..." So here, it mentions that when the winds reach 75 mph or more, they are called hurricanes. Looking at the options, option D says "When its winds reach 75 miles per hour." That seems to match. Let me check the other options to be sure. Option A says when it begins in the Atlantic and Caribbean seas. The passage says that many storms are born there, but only some become hurricanes based on wind speed. So A is not correct because just being in those regions isn't the defining factor. Option B is when it hits the coastline. The passage mentions that several hurricanes usually make their way to the coast, but the definition of a hurricane is based on wind speed, not when it hits land. Option C is when it's more than 75 miles wide. The passage talks about wind speed (75 mph) not width. So C
